coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-based antifungal, superhydrophobic and UV-resistant coating for wood materials
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoqi
Zhang, Shaodi
Zhang, Zhenxin
Cao, Jinzhen
coredata.dc:description: Wood is a sustainable and widely used material in construction. However, it is vulnerable to deterioration agents including fungi, ultraviolet (UV) light, and water, etc. Application of coating on wood surface is the most convenient approach to alleviate these attacks. In this work, a carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based antifungal, UV-resistant, and superhydrophobic coating (PCO) for wood surface was designed in a two-step method by first applying polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-CQDs composite and then octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) water emulsion. CQDs in PVA-CQDs composite not only provide anti-fungal properties but also bond the coating tightly with wood via hydrogen and covalent bonds. OTS treatment created a rugged surface with hierarchical roughness and hydrophobic functional groups, resulting in superhydrophobicity (water contact angle > 150°) of PCO coated wood. After PCO coating, the wood surface can completely prevent microbial attacks from Trametes versicolor (Tv), Postia placenta (Pp) and Aspergillus niger (An). Moreover, this coating can be easily applied onto various wood substrates, and exhibited excellent durability against weathering and abrasion. This work provides a potential technology to develop green and efficacious coating for wood materials based on CQDs, which shows significant prospect in practical use.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wood
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Coating
Durability
Weathering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs, PVA-CQDs, and PCO film
Fungal resistance of PCO coated wood
Characterizations of PCO and its components
Fabrication and characterization of PCO coating

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the design and assembly of PCO coating on wood surface. a. The two-step coating of PCO composed of CQDs, PVA and OTS. b. Anti-fungal, superhydrophobic and UV-resistant properties of PCO coated wood materials.

Characterizations of PVA-CQDs and PCO. a. Digital photos of PVA solution, PVA-CQDs solution, PVA film, and PVA-CQDs composite film. b-c). AFM of PVA film, and PVA-CQDs composite film. d. XRD of PVA and PVA-CQDs at room temperature. e. FTIR spectra of PVA and PVA-CQDs at room temperature. f. Stress-strain curves of PVA and the pristine PVA-CQDs (VCQDs /VPVA=0.05). g. Digital photos of PCO coating. h. SEM of PCO. d-e). AFM of PCO. f. FTIR spectra of PVA and PCO at room temperature.

Characterizations of PCO coated wood. a. Digital photos of PVA and PCO coated wood. b-d). SEM of PCO coated wood. e. The adhesion between wood and PVA or PCO coatings. f. Digital photos showing the interfaces between wood and PVA or PCO coatings after peel test, using a 180° peeling method. g. FTIR spectra of wood, PCO and wood+PCO at room temperature. h. The bonding mechanism between PCO and wood.

Antifungal performance of PVA, PVA-CQDs, and PCO and the coated wood. a. Anti-fungal properties of PVA and PVA-CQDsx, x = 1–5. b. Colony diameter on PDA with PVA and PVA-CQDsx, with Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of fungi cells before and after CQDs attack. Scale bar: 0.5 μm c-e). The ESR (·OH, ·O2 -, PFR) of CQDs under xenon lamp. f). The antifungal performance of PVA, PVA-CQDs, and PCO coated wood against Trametes versicolor (Tv).

Superhydrophobicity performances of PCO coated wood. a. WCAs of longitudinal, radical and tangential surface of hardwood and softwood. b. Superhydrophobicity and self-cleaning property of wood coated with PCO. c. WCAs of PCO coated wood and PCO coated wood after 5 abrasion cycles. d-e). WCAs of PVA-CQDs coated hardwood, softwood, bamboo, and bamboo scrimber.

UV-resistant performance of PCO coated wood as compared to PVA coated wood. a. Digital photos of pristine wood, PVA and PCO coated wood before and after weathering test. b. Changes in pristine wood, PVA-CQDs coated wood, and PCO coated wood of appearance total color change (∆E) with UV irradiation time. c. WCA of pristine wood, PVA-CQDs coated wood, and PCO coated wood after accelerated weathering test. d. The adhesion between wood and coating (PVA, PCO) after 600 h accelerated weathering test.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-based antifungal, superhydrophobic and UV-resistant coating for wood materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Efficient Production of Forest Resources & MOE Key Laboratory of Wooden Material Science and Application, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Efficient Production of Forest Resources & MOE Key Laboratory of Wooden Material Science and Application, Beijing Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of Wooden Material Science and Application, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wood is a sustainable and widely used material in construction. However, it is vulnerable to deterioration agents including fungi, ultraviolet (UV) light, and water, etc. Application of coating on wood surface is the most convenient approach to alleviate these attacks. In this work, a carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based antifungal, UV-resistant, and superhydrophobic coating (PCO) for wood surface was designed in a two-step method by first applying polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-CQDs composite and then octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) water emulsion. CQDs in PVA-CQDs composite not only provide anti-fungal properties but also bond the coating tightly with wood via hydrogen and covalent bonds. OTS treatment created a rugged surface with hierarchical roughness and hydrophobic functional groups, resulting in superhydrophobicity (water contact angle > 150°) of PCO coated wood. After PCO coating, the wood surface can completely prevent microbial attacks from Trametes versicolor (Tv), Postia placenta (Pp) and Aspergillus niger (An). Moreover, this coating can be easily applied onto various wood substrates, and exhibited excellent durability against weathering and abrasion. This work provides a potential technology to develop green and efficacious coating for wood materials based on CQDs, which shows significant prospect in practical use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wood has been a primary material in construction for millennia, owing to its abundance, ease of processing, and excellent mechanical properties. Nowadays, the sustainability of wood makes it a promising alternative to energy-consuming building materials such as steel and concrete, and has attracted significant attention in the research fields of construction and building materials. However, as a natural material, wood is susceptible to various fungi, such as brown rot and white rot fungi. Fungi attack wood components, leading to discoloration and deterioration of wood and affect the structural integrity [1,2]. Therefore, the antifungal treatments are essential for preventing wood from degradation and prolonging its service life in building and construction applications.

Antifungal agents have been developed to endow wood with fungal resistance by impregnation or coating [3]. Traditional methods often involve the use of chemical preservatives, such as copper-based compounds (e.g., copper azole), quaternary ammonium salts, and chromium-based chemicals [4]. However, these antifungal agents can pose severe environmental and health risks, as many of the chemicals used are toxic and may leach into the environment. Literature has reported that some natural polymers (such as chitosan) and plant derivatives (including phenolic compounds, alkaloids, terpenoids) have broad biological activity against fungi [5–8]. Chitosan is the most studied natural polymer that has been used for the antifungal treatment of wood [9]. Facchi et.al. found that high molar mass chitosan can form an antifungal coating on wood, and this chitosan film can effectively inhibit growth of Neolentinus lepideus and Trametes versicolor, two typical wood decay fungi [10]. However, the production of these natural polymers or plant derivatives is very low, which makes the practical application of them in wood products impossible for now. Therefore, developing environmental friendly antifungal agents suitable for practical applications is essential for the green wood preservation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their unique physicochemical properties, such as high surface area, excellent optical characteristics, and biocompatibility. More importantly, CQDs are ease of synthesis, and the raw materials of them are have extensive supply sources and easy accessibility [11]. Previous studies found that CQDs possess exceptional antibacterial properties due to its small particle size, large surface area, and abundance of functional groups [12,13]. In the antibacterial applications, CQDs with specific functional groups can form strong electrostatic interactions with bacterial cell membranes [14]. The nano-size CQDs (∼2 nm) are proved to be more efficient in killing bacterial due to facilitated internalization in the cells and the distribution of CQDs in cell membranes [15]. Furthermore, upon light exposure, CQDs are capable of generating reactive oxygen species (ROS), which cause cell wall damage to bacterial cells [16]. In our recent work, Chinese herb-Forsythia derived CQDs were synthesized in one-pot method [17]. It was found that Forsythia derived CQDs exhibited excellent resistance against C. versicolor and G. trabeum, two typical wood decay fungal. The treated wood showed significantly reduced weight loss after 12 weeks of antifungal test. However, the antifungal mechanisms of CQDs have not been understood. Moreover, due to the water solubility, the leaching of CQDs should be concerned yet has not been considered.

Other than fungi, water and ultraviolet (UV) from sunlight are also key factors that determine the service life of wood. Wood can absorb and desorb water from the environment and lead to swelling and shrinking, which will cause cracks. Water facilitates fungal growth, further accelerating wood decay [18]. When irradiated under UV light, photo degradation of wood occurred, resulting in significant physical or chemical change [19]. For example, the color of wood changes, deformation and cracks appear on the surface, and the degradation of lignin occurs [20]. It was proved that photo degradation could accelerate the decay process of wood and bamboo [21,22]. Therefore, hydrophobic and UV-proofing treatments for wood are very important for improving its durability.

To address these problems and promote the practical application of CQDs in wood preservation, in this work, we prepared an antifungal, and super-hydrophobic coating for wood materials based on CQDs. CQDs were synthesized and mixed with PVA to form a PVA-CQDs composite and coated on wood. Then, PVA-CQDs coated wood was treated by octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) - water emulsion to obtain a PVA-CQDs-OTS (PCO) coating ( Fig. 1a). The antifungal mechanisms of CQDs were first studied by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) spectroscopic measurements. Then, antifungal and hydrophobic performances of PCO coated wood were investigated, and the mechanical stability, weathering durability, and generality of the PCO coating were studied (Fig. 1b). The findings of this work will shed lights in developing novel multifunctional coatings for enhancing wood durability using eco-friendly and low cost CQDs.

All chemicals and biological reagents (urea, citric acid, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 105), octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS, ≥90 %), hexane, potato dextrose agar (PDA), 2.5 % glutaraldehyde, anhydrous ethanol, phosphate buffer solution, acetone) were all AR grade and purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Sapwood of cathay poplar (Populus cathayana Rehd.) and southern pine (Pinus spp.) free of defects (such as knots and decay) were used as wood substrates. The dimensions of the specimens were 20 × 20 × 10 mm3 and 75 × 20 × 3 mm3 (longitudinal × radial × tangential).

Three typical fungi representing divergent fungal lifestyles were selected as the test fungi species in this study: Trametes versicolor (T. versicolor, Tv, CGMCC No. 5.1500, white-rot fungus) and Aspergillus niger (A. niger, An, CGMCC No.3.2915, mould) were purchased from Institute of Microbiology, Beijing Forestry University; Postia placenta (P. placenta, Pp, CFCC No. 5608, brown-rot fungus) was purchased from China General Microbiological Culture Collection Center.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs, PVA-CQDs, and PCO film
ce:section.ce:para: A clear solution was obtained by dissolving 6.0 g urea and 0.6 g citric acid in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was placed in a conical flask for open reaction under microwave at 500 W for 5 min. The obtained product was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and sonicated for about 20 min. Insoluble matter was removed through centrifugation (10000 rmp, 10 min). After that, brown solution of CQDs was obtained. The solution was further dialyzed for 48 h (500 Da dialysis bag) with deionized water (changed every 24 h). After freeze-drying the dialyzed solution in a freeze-dryer (LGJ-10, Songyuan, China) at −40 ℃ for 48 h, CQDs were obtained. PVA solution was prepared by mixing 50.0 g of PVA in 150 mL of deionized water followed by heating it in a water bath (95 ℃) for 120 min. PVA-CQDs was prepared by mixing certain amount of deionized water and CQDs solution with PVA solution with vigorous stirring for more than 5 min. After dried in ambient environment in a petri dish or a plastic tube, PVA-CQDs films were obtained. PVA-CQDs with different contents of CQDs (0.06, 0.12, 0.18, 0.24, 0.36 mg/mL) were denoted as PVA-CQDsx, x = 1–5. 200 μL water was added to 10 mL OTS in a glass tube. The tube was then caped and immediately put on a vortex mixer at 3200 rpm for 10 s, followed by sonication for 10 s and another round of vortex mixing for 10 s. After 2 h, 100 mL hexane was added and mixed by shaking. PCO film was prepared by treating PVA-CQDs film using OTS emulsion following the reported method [23]. The PCO prepared based on PVA-CQDsx were denoted as PCOx, x = 1–5.

Morphologies and nano-structure were investigated by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8010, Japan), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, America), and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Bruker Multimode 8, Germany). Raman spectra was carried out using a Raman spectrometer (Ranishaw inVia, UK). The functional groups of samples were recorded using Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet IS10, USA) in the wavelength range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a 2 cm−1 resolution and an accumulation of 32 scans. Water contact angle (WCA) was recorded using a contact angle meter (SL200KS, Kino, USA). A micro injector was used in a contact angle measuring instrument to drop 3 μL of water droplets onto a solid surface. Six different locations for WCA testing for each sample. To test the Reactive oxygen species (ROS) of samples, Electron Spin Resonance Spectroscopic (ESR) measurements were carried out at ambient temperature using a Bruker EMXplus-6/1 spectrometer (Germany) following reported method [24]. Two time points were taken [5 minutes of darkness; 10 min of light exposure (Xenon-lamp: 500 W)]. The morphology of untreated and CQDs treated fungi (Tv) cells with or without CQDs was observed on biological scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU8010, Japan).

Tensile strength of samples was tested using a universal mechanical testing machine (WDW-T100, Tianchen, China) with a 100 mm/min according to GB 1447–2005. Dog-Bones shape samples were prepared for the test. The 180° peel strength of the coating was tested referring to GB/T 2792–2014, with a peeling speed of 200 mm/min from the substrate surface. Six replicates were measured in each group.

Cultured Tv was added to the tissue grinder, a small amount of deionized water was slowly added for grinding, and the ground fungal solution was transferred to a triangular flask. Then, an appropriate amount of water was added and shaken for 10 min. 200 μL of fungal solution was taken and brushed uniformly on the PDA medium. To determine the antifungal activity of PVA-CQDs, a PVA-CQDs film with diameter of 5 mm and thickness of 2 mm was placed on a PDA medium coated with fungal. Then the PDA mediums were incubated in a climate chamber with a temperature of 28 ℃ and a relative humidity of 85 % for 7 days. The mycelium diameter of the fungus was measured on the 7th day [25]. The diameter of the antifungal circle denotes the antifungal effect of the gel block.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fungal resistance of PCO coated wood
ce:section.ce:para:
Fungal resistance of PCO coated wood was tested according to Chinese standard GB/T 13942.1–2009. PCO coated wood prepared by dipping in PVA-CQDs composite solution, followed by drying and dipping in OTS emulsion. Then the wood specimens were dried in a chamber at 25 ℃ for 48 h. The PCO coated wood specimens were placed on feeding wood fully cover by mycelia, which were further incubated at 27 ℃ and 80 % of relative humidity (RH) for 12 weeks. After 12 weeks of test, the mycelia on the wood surface were carefully cleaned then dried at 103 ± 2 ℃ to a constant weight. The mass loss was determined according to formula (1):
(1)
Mass
loss
rate ( % )
=
W 1 − W 2
W 1
× 100
Where W1 and W2 represent the weight of wood specimens before and after the fungal resistance test, respectively.

Cultured An was added to the tissue grinder, a small amount of deionized water was slowly added for grinding, and the ground fungal solution was transferred to a triangular flask. Then, an appropriate amount of water was added and shake for 10 min. 200 μL of fungal solution was taken and brushed uniformly on the PDA medium. Then the PDA mediums were incubated in a climate chamber with a temperature of 28 ℃ and a relative humidity of 85 % for 2 days. The mycelium diameter of the fungus was measured on the 7th day [26]. Then the PCO coated wood specimens were placed on PDA fully cover by mycelia, which were further incubated at 27 ℃ and 80 % of relative humidity (RH) for 4 weeks.
The weathering test was conducted in an accelerated weathering tester (QUV/Spray, Q-Lab Corporation, USA) according to ASTM G 154 for 600 h following the reported study [27].

The WCA of weathered samples were tested. The surface color of samples was measured with a chroma meter (Datacolor DF 110, Shenzhen 3NH Technology Co., Ltd, Shenzhen, China) according to the CIE LAB color system. L* , a* , and b* were recorded. Among them, L* represents signifies lightness, the chromaticity coordinates along the red-green index, and b* represents the chromaticity coordinates along the red-green index. Measure 6 samples per group, take six random positions on the surface of each sample, and calculate their average value. The total color change (Δ E * ) is calculated according to Eq. (2).
(2)
∆ E =
∆ L
* 2
+
∆ a
* 2
+
∆ b
* 2
Among them, Δ L * , Δ a * , and Δ b * are the final values and the initial values of L* , a* , and b* , respectively.

Sandpaper abrasion test was used to test the mechanical stability of PCO coating. A 400 mesh sandpaper was placed on wood sample and pressed by a 100 g weight. An abrasion cycle was defined as pulling the sandpaper and make it move for 10 cm, then move back. The WCA of samples after 5 abrasion cycles were tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of PCO and its components
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S1a–b, CQDs dispersed well in water, and had a particle size of 1–3 nm. The results of FTIR and Raman spectrum indicated the successful synthesis of nano-sized, nitrogen-doped CQDs with abundant surfaced defects and functional groups via microwave-assisted heating (Fig. S1c–d) [28].

By mixing CQDs with PVA solution and air-drying at ambient temperature, PVA-CQDs composite film was observed ( Fig. 2a). Due to the small particle size and well distribution of CQDs, the as-obtained PVA-CQD film exhibited high transparency of 93.3 % and low reflectance (Fig. 2a, Fig. S2). Morphologies of PVA and PVA-CQDs coating were investigated by AFM. The surface of the PVA coating was smooth and PVA-CQDs coating was rugged (Fig. 2b–c). CQDs can be observed on the surface of PVA-CQDs, the height of the PVA coating has been increased by approximately 4 nm. XRD patterns of PVA and PVA-CQDs was shown in Fig. 2d. As seen with increasing CQDs nanoparticles concentration, the peak intensity corresponded to PVA at 2θ= 20° increases accordingly. This may be due to the increase hydrogen bonds between CQDs and the molecular chains of the PVA and thus increasing the crystallinity. In the FTIR spectra of PVA-CQDs, absorption peaks at 3300, 1330, and 1080 cm−1 confirm the presence of -OH, C-H, and C-O groups in PVA, respectively (Fig. 2e). Also, an increase of band at 1630 cm−1 reflecting OC-NH stretching is also observed [29–31], indicating the covalent bonding between CQDs. The broad band at 3000–3600 cm−1 was ascribed to NH2/OH groups and hydrogen bonds. Due to the presence of abundant -NH2 groups in CQDs, the peak shift from 3265 cm−1 for PVA to 3336 cm−1 for PVA-CQDs. And this band became broader, indicating the presence of hydrogen bonds between PVA and CQDs. Due to the abundant hydrogen bonds between PVA and CQDs, and the good distribution of nano-sized CQDs in PVA matrix, the tensile strength of PVA increased from 2.0 ± 0.1 MPa to 2.5 ± 0.96 MPa after CQDs addition, and the elongation to break of PVA-CQDs composite increased significantly (Fig. 2f). The shear strength of PVA-CQDs composite bonded three-layered plywood was 20.7 % increased compared with PVA bonded plywood (Fig. S3), which also supported the strong bonding between PVA and CQDs.

Morphologies of PCO coating were investigated by SEM and AFM (Fig. 2g-e). The surface of the PCO coating was rugged, with height difference of ∼1 μm. Microparticles can be observed on the surface, and they were consisted of aggregated nanoparticles with a diameter of 100–300 nm. FTIR spectra of PCO coating were shown in Fig. 2f. The broad band at 3200–3600 cm−1 reduced significantly due to reaction between the -OH in PVA and the silanol groups of OTS. Two distinct bands at 2915 and 2850 cm−1 belong to the to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of -CH2 groups from the long chain alkyl group of OTS. The peak at 1012 and 890 cm−1 associated with the Si-O-Si bond. And the new peak at 800 cm−1 could be attributed to Si-O group. A weak shoulder peak at 956 cm−1 attributed to Si-O-C bond appeared, further confirmed the reaction between PVA and OTS. The hierarchical roughness along with alkyl groups should endow the coating with good hydrophobicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of PCO coating
ce:section.ce:para: PCO coating on wood was prepared by dipping wood blocks in PVA-CQDs composite, followed by drying and dipping in OTS emulsion. The coated wood exhibited natural color and texture, maintaining its pristine aesthetic ( Fig. 3a). Morphologies of PCO coating was investigated by SEM and AFM, and the surface of the PCO coating was rugged (Fig. 3b, Fig. S4). Microparticles can be observed on the surface, and they were consisted of aggregated nanoparticles with a diameter of 100–300 nm (Fig. 3c).

As shown in Fig. 3d, the thickness of the PCO coating is about 74 μm, which is closely adhered with the wood components. Glue nail can also be observed. The adhesion between wood and PCO coating were investigated by peel test (Fig. 3e). Pure PVA coated wood showed a peel strength of 0.48 ± 0.07 N/m2, and it increased gradually to the maximum of 0.96 ± 0.05 N/m2 when CQDs content was 0.18 mg/mL. And clear wood failure showed up in PCO coated wood (Fig. 3f), indicating strong adhesion between wood and PCO coating. This could attribute to abundant hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding between the coating and wood components, as well as the good penetration of nano-sized CQDs and PVA molecules in wood cell wall through micropores [17,32]. The FTIR spectra of wood and PCO coated wood were shown in Fig. 3g. Other than the Si-O-Si bonds and -CH2 groups from OTS, a red shift of the band at 3425 cm−1 could be observed, indicating new hydrogen bonds. Additionally, a weak peak at 1645 cm−1 appeared in PCO coated wood, probably belonged to the NH2-CO bonds from the reaction between -NH2 groups in CQDs and carboxyl groups in wood (hemicellulose and lignin). The bonding between PCO and wood was illustrated in Fig. 3h.

The antifungal properties of PVA-CQDsx were tested through in vitro antifungal testing on PDA medium, Pp was chosen as a case in point and the results were shown in Fig. 4. PVA treated group was completely occupied by Pp hyphae, indicating that pure PVA has no antifungal activity. With the addition of CQDs, the diameter of fungal colony decreased (Fig. 4a-b). The colony completely disappeared when CQDs concentration reached to 180 ppm, meaning the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of CQDs is 180 ppm for Pp. This is comparable to or better than commercial heavy metal-containing antifungal agents for wood products [33]. OTS has no antifungal effect, indicating that the antifungal properties of the PCO coating are mainly provided by CQDs. The morphologies of untreated and CQDs treated fungal cell were shown in insert map of Fig. 4b. Untreated hyphae exhibited smooth and straight shape, while CQDs treated Pp showed a twisted hyphae shape with wrinkled surface, suggesting CQDs affected the functions of fungal cell membrane/wall [34].

The radical oxygen species (ROS) generated by CQDs were investigated via ESR technique. As shown in Fig. 4c–e, ROS including hydroxyl (·OH), superoxide radical (·O2 -), and persistent free radicals (PFR) were produced under exposure of light for 10 min. The mechanism by which CQDs generate ROS primarily relies on their photocatalytic properties. Under light irradiation, CQDs absorb photon energy, exciting electrons to transition from the valence band to the conduction band, thereby forming electron-hole pairs. These electrons and holes can react with surrounding water molecules or oxygen, generating ROS. Furthermore, functional groups on the surface of CQDs (e.g., carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) can participate in electron transfer processes, further enhancing ROS generation. These ROS will attack fungal cells, resulting in damage to cellular lipids, proteins, and DNA, and further improved fungicidal ability of CQDs [35]. In summary, CQDs could kill fungal cell via adhering and penetrating cell membrane to disrupt the fungal cell metabolism, and via generating destructive ROS under light exposure.

We further tested the antifungal performance of PVA, PVA-CQDs, and PCO coated wood using Tv. Pristine wood fully covered by fungal hypaes after the test (Fig. 4f). In comparison, no obvious fungal colonies can be found on the surface of PCO coated wood, showing excellent antifungal performance. The mass loss of pristine wood reached 54.05 ± 8.81 % and 49.02 ± 5.24 % infected by Pp and Tv, respectively (Fig. S5). And the mass losses of PCO coated wood after Pp and Tv tests reduced to 10.31 ± 1.26 % and 16.77 ± 0.78 %, respectively. PCO coated wood also exhibited 100 % preventing efficiency against An, an ubiquitous mildew on wood materials (Fig. S5). This can be primarily attributed to the hydrophobic nature of the sample surface and the antifungal efficacy of CQDs [36].

Due to the hierarchical roughness and alkyl chains of the PCO surface, PCO coated wood exhibited superhydrophobicity [37]. As shown in Fig. 5a, WCAs of longitudinal, radical and tangential surface of hardwood and softwood were higher than 150° and the sliding angle was lower than 5°. Additionally, the PCO coated wood showed self-cleaning property (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, mechanical stability of the coating was studied by sandpaper abrasion test. The PCO coating still exhibited hydrophobicity (WCA =156°) after 5 abrasion cycles (Fig. 5c), showing good abrasion resistance. The durability could attribute to the tight bonding between wood and PCO coating. Additionally, PCO coating can be applied on various wood products, including hardwood, softwood, bamboo, and bamboo scrimber, and showing superhydrophobicity (Fig. 5d–e), indicating its generality.

In practical scenarios, especially outdoors, wood products affected by many factors including water, light, heat, and abrasion, and undergoes deterioration. Therefore, durability of coatings is a significant issue that should be concerned. The weathering resistance of PCO coated wood was investigated using accelerated weathering test ( Fig. 6a). After 600 h of test, color difference distribution on PCO coated wood was much lower than pristine wood, and the ΔE of PCO coated wood was lower than that of untreated or PVA coated wood (Fig. 6b, Fig. S6), probably due to the UV-absorbing effect of CQDs [38]. WCA of pristine wood and PVA coated wood gradually decreased rapidly during the weathering test, and PCO coated wood remained superhydrophobic (WCA>150°, Fig. 6c, Movies S1), indicating excellent weathering durability.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.137012.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Movies S1.
Movies S1

The adhesion between wood and PCO coating after 600 h of test were investigated by peel test (Fig. 6d). Pure PVA coated wood showed poor peel strength of 0 N/m2. On the contrary, strong peel strength between wood and PCO coating due to its excellent weathering durability.

In this work, an antifungal, superhydrophobic, and UV-resistant coating for wood surface, PCO, was applied via a two-step dipping method. CQDs as a novel antifungal agent was combined with PVA to form PVA-CQDs composite, which was coated in the first step and adhered firmly on wood surface via strong hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding to provide both mechanical stability and antifungal property. Further deposition of OTS on PVA-CQDs composite generated a rugged surface with hierarchical roughness and abundant alkyl chains, leading to superhydrophobicity as demonstrated by a WCA of 162° and a sliding angle of 5°. The PCO coated wood also exhibited a mass loss of only16.77 % under exposure to Tv, which was much lower than pristine wood. The PCO coating is also UV-resistant and abrasion-resistant, by maintaining superhydrophobicity after 5 abrasion cycles and 600 h accelerated weathering test. This work provides strategy for the development of antifungal and superhdrophobic durable coatings based on CQDs for wood materials for prolong its service life in construction and building applications.

Cao Jinzhen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhang Zhenxin: Visualization. Zhang Shaodi: Investigation. Zhao Xiaoqi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study is supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFD2200502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.137012.
